topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt like sandpaper under my fingertips as I unfolded the document. Every line, every carefully chosen anecdote about my childhood, my supposed triumphs, my very essence, was there. It was *my* life, meticulously chronicled, yet it was utterly alien. My breath hitched, a sharp, involuntary intake of air that burned in my chest. I stared at the elegant script, the details about my first job, the supposed epiphany on that fateful trip—they were all twisted, subtly rearranged. A tremor started in my hands, making the paper flutter precariously. "This is impossible," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the silence of my apartment. I slammed the paper onto the desk, the sharp crack echoing through the room. I began pacing, my movements jerky and agitated, running a hand through my hair as if trying to physically shake the falsehood out of my skin. I needed answers, and the silence of the room was suffocating with unanswered questions.

[story 2]

When the courier delivered the package, the weight of it felt heavy, ominous. Inside, nestled in tissue paper, was a bound volume—my life story. Opening it was like being blindsided by a physical blow. My eyes scanned the narrative, and a cold, hard knot tightened in my stomach. The prose painted a picture of someone I was, but the nuances were wrong; the motivations were skewed. A slow, building heat started to rise in my neck, making my skin prickle. I snatched the book, my knuckles white, and flipped frantically through the pages, searching for a discrepancy, a misplaced fact, anything to prove it was fabricated. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles strain in my face. "Who authorized this?" I demanded, my voice low, vibrating with a sudden, intense need for accountability. I threw the book onto the floor, the sound sharp and final, and stood there, breathing heavily, the air suddenly too thin to sustain me.

[story 3]

I sat at the antique mahogany table, the afternoon light catching the sheen of the printed biography. My gaze swept over the text, and a wave of profound disbelief washed over me, quickly morphing into something hot and corrosive. It wasn't just inaccurate; it felt like a deliberate, malicious sculpting of my existence. I leaned forward, my elbows digging into the wood, my entire posture radiating a coiled tension. My lips pressed into a thin, white line. "They didn't just write this," I articulated slowly, each word measured and laced with simmering fury. My fingers flexed against the edge of the table until the skin beneath felt tight and stretched. I stood abruptly, knocking my chair back slightly. I began to walk the perimeter of the room, my steps quick and uneven, a restless energy vibrating through my limbs. Every corner I turned brought a fresh surge of indignation, a need to confront the source of this deception head-on.
